food_subgroup: Traditional Chinese sweet soup / tong sui dessert with lotus seeds

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lotus seed food product class; no specific entry for Hunan-style slow-cooked lotus seeds as a prepared dessert dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers lotus seeds as a raw/processed ingredient; no specific code for the Hunan slow-cooked dessert preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for lotus seeds or Hunan lotus seed dessert as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 200 g, presented as a bowl of slow-cooked lotus seeds in a lightly sweetened broth.

nutrient_source.title: Lotus seeds, dried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 16109)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on USDA FoodData Central data for dried lotus seeds (Nelumbo nucifera), adapted to reflect the cooked/hydrated state as served in this dish. Cooked lotus seeds have approximately 60–65% moisture; values adjusted accordingly. Rock sugar contributes additional carbohydrate and energy; a modest sugar addition of approximately 8 g per 100 g of final dish is incorporated based on standard tong sui preparation ratios. Per-100g values represent the final cooked dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Foster-Powell et al. / Atkinson et al.; lotus seeds analogous estimate based on legume/seed GI data from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Hunan-style slow-cooked lotus seeds was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Lotus seeds are starchy seeds with a high resistant starch and amylose content, comparable to legumes and other low-GI starchy seeds. An analogous GI of 47 is estimated based on published GI values for cooked lotus seeds and similar starchy legume seeds (GI range 40–54) in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care, 2008). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 37.0) / 100 ≈ 17.4, rounded to 17.2 using 18.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 200 g serving = 37 g carbohydrate; GL = (47 × 37) / 100 = 17.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~5 mg per 100 g) with beneficial potassium (~180 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Hunan lotus seeds are very low in sodium and provide potassium, making them a suitable occasional dessert for individuals managing hypertension, provided added sugar is kept moderate.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~17 per 200 g serving) due to starch and added rock sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (estimated ~47) of lotus seeds is favourable for glycaemic control, but the added rock sugar in this preparation raises the glycemic load to moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a bowl and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and presence of potassium and magnesium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hunan lotus seeds are very low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, with potassium and magnesium supporting vascular function; they represent a heart-friendly dessert option when consumed without excessive added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hunan Lotus Seeds (湖南莲子, Húnán liánzǐ) is a traditional Chinese sweet dessert soup (tong sui) featuring lotus seeds sourced from Hunan Province, one of China's most celebrated lotus-growing regions. The dish is prepared by slow-cooking plump, dried lotus seeds in water with rock sugar until the seeds become tender and slightly powdery in texture, releasing a delicate, subtly sweet, and mildly earthy fragrance. The resulting broth is lightly sweetened and clear to pale golden in colour. Lotus seeds (Nelumbo nucifera) are a nutrient-dense starchy seed rich in complex carbohydrates, plant protein (~2.8 g per 100 g cooked), dietary fibre, B vitamins (thiamine, B6, folate), potassium, phosphorus, and magnesium. They are naturally low in fat and sodium. The dish has a low glycemic index (estimated ~47) due to the high resistant starch and amylose content of lotus seeds, though the addition of rock sugar raises the glycemic load to moderate per standard serving. Clinically, this dessert is suitable for individuals managing hypertension due to its very low sodium and beneficial potassium content. For Type 2 Diabetes management, portion control is advised given the moderate glycemic load. The dish is cholesterol-free and very low in saturated fat, making it appropriate for cardiovascular health. In Chinese culinary tradition, lotus seeds are prized for their cooling and calming properties and are commonly served as a restorative dessert or snack. This dish is served at RUXU 入续 糖水铺与包 in Singapore and is representative of the Hunan tong sui tradition.
common_user_queries: Are lotus seeds good for diabetes?
Hunan lotus seeds GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: No exact match for Hunan-style slow-cooked lotus seeds as a prepared dish was found in any primary approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central dried lotus seed data, adjusted for the cooked state and rock sugar addition. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for comparable starchy seeds and legumes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent the final cooked dish (lotus seeds slow-cooked with rock sugar) per 100 g as served. Dried lotus seed USDA data was adjusted for approximately 65% moisture in the cooked state and a rock sugar contribution of ~8 g sugar per 100 g final dish. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported for cooked lotus seeds in the USDA source used. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as lotus seeds contain negligible vitamin D and it is not reported in the USDA source. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for this food. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for cooked lotus seeds. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as lotus seeds are a plant food containing no vitamin B12; confirmed zero for plant-origin foods. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as lotus seeds contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA data. 8. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as lotus seeds are a plant food; confirmed zero per USDA data. 9. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific Hunan preparation was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 10. Glycemic load per serving (200 g): (47 × 37.0 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 17.4, reported as 17.2 after rounding. 11. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 is used as closest parent for lotus seed food product; exact prepared-dish code does not exist. 12. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as closest parent for lotus seeds ingredient; no prepared-dish code exists in FoodEx2 for this dish.
